channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Videos Detail Moments Surrounding Fatal ICE Shooting in Biddeford, Maine
item.description: Footage obtained by The New York Times shows ICE agents in the moments before and after the killing of Joan Sebastian Guerrero. The circumstances of the shooting remain unclear.
item.dc:creator: Allison McCann, Devon Lum, Tim Ouillette, Aric Toler and Leanne Abraham

item.title: One Dead and Three Missing in San Francisco Bay After Boat Capsizes Near Alcatraz
item.description: Sixteen other passengers were rescued after the pleasure boat sank in the San Francisco Bay on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: The boat stalled about 600 yards from Alcatraz Island.

item.title: U.S. War Against Iran Enters a New Phase
item.description: As President Trump resumes his war, the focus is now on the Strait of Hormuz. But it remains unclear how far the U.S. military will go to exert control.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Eric Schmitt and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday. The latest phase of the U.S. military campaign in Iran has a new focus, but not necessarily a clearer strategy.

item.title: As Confirmation Hearing Nears, Todd Blanche’s Role in Epstein Case Draws Scrutiny
item.description: Todd Blanche’s role in interviewing Ghislaine Maxwell, a longtime associate of the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, will be front and center as he seeks to become attorney general.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, last month. As the Justice Department’s No. 2 official, he oversaw the release of the Epstein files.

item.title: At War With Iran Again, Trump Finds an Opponent He Cannot Easily Dominate
item.description: The president is accustomed to forcing other countries to bend to his will. But he is struggling to come up with a strategy to extract Iranian concessions as the cease-fire he brokered collapses.
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A banner in Tehran last week threatening President Trump.

item.title: House Votes for Permanent Daylight Saving Time
item.description: House Republicans and Democrats united in favor of a bill to eliminate semiannual clock-changing, but it faces an uncertain fate in the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Diaz
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Capitol in March 2023, when then-Senator Marco Rubio reintroduced the Sunshine Protection Act, to permanently enact Daylight Saving Time and eliminate the twice-a-year clock changes.

item.title: An ICE Shooting in Maine Puts Pressure on Senator Susan Collins
item.description: The Democrats hoping to challenge Ms. Collins, a vulnerable Republican, have seized on the fatal incident, drawing attention to her record on immigration matters.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: “It’s disappointing to see potential candidates use a tragedy to try to advance a political agenda,” Senator Susan Collins told reporters at the U.S. Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: Colombian Immigrant Killed by ICE in Maine Had Legal Status, Father Says
item.description: The father of Joan Sebastian Guerrero, the man fatally shot by a federal immigration agent on Monday, said his son had been working two jobs to support his wife and daughter.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales, Jenna Russell and Jacey Fortin
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial for Joan Sebastian Guerrero at the intersection where he was shot by ICE in Biddeford, Maine.

item.title: ICE Shootings Put Spotlight on Lack of Body Cameras
item.description: Kristi Noem, the former homeland security secretary, said five months ago that the Trump administration would “rapidly” deploy body cameras to immigration agents across the country. About half still don’t have them.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:description: Law enforcement officers at the scene of the ICE shooting in Biddeford, Maine, on Tuesday.

item.title: Opportunities Narrow for Women as Hegseth Blocks More Promotions
item.description: For the first time in more than a decade, no active-duty female Navy officers are likely to be promoted to admiral this year.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has repeatedly claimed that the military has focused too much on promoting minority and female officers, at the expense of white men.

item.title: Lindsey Graham’s Sister, Darline Graham, Is Sworn In to Finish His Senate Term
item.description: Three days after Lindsey Graham died, his younger sister Darline Graham took the oath to hold his seat until his term ends in January.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Diaz
item.media:description: Darline Graham being sworn in by Senator Charles E. Grassley on Tuesday as her husband, Larry Nordone, holds a Bible.

item.title: Trump Administration Subpoenas Law Firms, Escalating Pressure Campaign
item.description: The uneasy truce between the administration and the law firms it cut deals with last year has erupted into a pitched legal battle behind closed doors.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt and Devlin Barrett
item.category: Wilmer Cutler Pickering Hale and Dorr LLP
item.media:description: The subpoenas — which were part of a civil dispute and not a criminal investigation — ask for all communications the firms had with Boris Epshteyn, President Trump’s personal lawyer.

item.title: ‘Sully,’ ‘Miracle on the Hudson’ Pilot, Announces Alzheimer’s Diagnosis
item.description: Captain Sullenberger, who is known as Sully, said that the disease was in its early stages, and that he was “in the beginning of this long journey.”
item.dc:creator: Alena Cerro
item.media:credit: Heidi Schumann for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chesley B. Sullenberger III, who is known as Sully, near his home in Danville, Calif., in 2009.

item.title: Lindsey Graham’s Death Leaves Fate of Russia Sanctions Bill Uncertain
item.description: Supporters in both parties hope the measure to penalize Russia, championed by Senator Lindsey Graham, will gain momentum in his honor.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:description: Senator Lindsey Graham spoke to reporters in a visit to Kyiv, Ukraine, last week, in his last public act.

item.title: Senate Democrats Block Defense Bill Over War in Iran
item.description: The vote reflected how the political debate over the conflict in the Middle East has sapped bipartisan support for a normally popular measure.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: The fate of the National Defense Authorization Act, a vital measure setting Pentagon policy and program funding, was left uncertain.

item.title: Top Tax Official to Leave Trump Administration
item.description: Ken Kies, who held two top jobs at the Treasury Department and the I.R.S., is leaving the government.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren

item.title: ‘We Love Iraq,’ Trump Tells New Prime Minister in Oval Office Meeting
item.description: The meeting comes as President Trump’s administration has been ratcheting up pressure on Baghdad to diminish Iran’s influence in Iraq.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: “We’re going to be doing a lot of deals,” President Trump said as he met with Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi in the Oval Office on Tuesday.

item.title: Takeaways From Justices Kagan and Barrett’s Congressional Testimony on Supreme Court Security
item.description: Elena Kagan and Amy Coney Barrett, the first Supreme Court justices to testify since 2019, talked about ethics and security threats during collegial hearings.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: Justices Elena Kagan and Amy Coney Barrett during a hearing at the Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Says His Thursday Speech Will Focus on Election Security and Voting Machines
item.description: The planned prime-time address comes as an administration task force has been working to declassify documents, including some related to elections, according to people familiar with the process.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Julian E. Barnes
item.media:description: President Trump has repeatedly made false claims that mail ballots are insecure and there is widespread voting by noncitizens.

item.title: Man in Florida Fatally Hit by Truck While Fleeing ICE, Official Says
item.description: Details were unclear on Tuesday about what exactly happened at a gas station in St. Augustine before the man was killed.
item.dc:creator: David Ovalle and Hamed Aleaziz

item.title: With World Cup Semifinals Starting During the U.S. Workday, Fans Get Creative
item.description: FIFA has scheduled both semifinal matches for 3 p.m. Eastern time on Tuesday and Wednesday, when many viewers in the United States will be working.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:description: Cesar Pratt, 45, said he would try to watch the World Cup semifinal matches while he was working.

item.title: Trump Paid $2 Million by South Korean Company Facing Trade Investigation
item.description: The payment illustrates the minefield Mr. Trump has created by maintaining personal financial ties with foreign businesses while he is in office.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton and John Yoon
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump maintains personal financial ties with nearly 30 different business ventures with counterparties worldwide.

item.title: ICE Ordered to Cease Most Vehicle Stops After Fatal Shootings in Maine and Houston
item.description: Agents fatally shot a man in Houston and another in coastal Maine, both in their vehicles. The killings were the latest in a string of ICE shootings during President Trump’s second term.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo, Hamed Aleaziz and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial for a Mexican immigrant, Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, at the spot where he was shot by an ICE agent in Houston.

item.title: Security threats led the Supreme Court to shut its front doors 16 years ago.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak

item.title: Justice Kagan Tells House Panel That ‘Threats Have Come Very Close’
item.description: In her prepared remarks, Justice Elena Kagan said the Supreme Court Police estimated a 38 percent increase in threats this year.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: Supreme Court Justices Elena Kagan and Amy Coney Barrett prepare to testify at the House Committee on Appropriations.

item.title: Threats against the justices have been rising precipitously.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: Officers outside the Supreme Court in Washington last month.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow

item.title: Why Supreme Court Justices Are Asking for More Security
item.description: Supreme Court justices are asking lawmakers on Capitol Hill to increase their 2027 budget, with most of the additional funding earmarked for security. Ann E. Marimow, a New York Times reporter, explains why the justices say these measures are necessary to protect them from rising threats.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow, Gilad Thaler, Nour Idriss, Jon Miller, Whitney Shefte, Stephanie Swart, Rafaela Balster and Nikolay Nikolov

item.title: In Rare Testimony, Supreme Court Justices Will Ask Congress for Security Funds
item.description: Elena Kagan and Amy Coney Barrett, the first justices to appear before lawmakers since 2019, are requesting millions of dollars to address security concerns amid rising threats.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s proposed $228 million budget includes funding to expand its in-house police force, which handles round-the-clock security at the justices’ homes, in addition to providing security when the justices travel outside the Washington area.

item.title: ‘I Am Frustrated’: Emails Show Blanche’s Role in Trump’s Retribution Campaign
item.description: The cooperation of Todd Blanche will be a flashpoint in his confirmation hearing to be attorney general on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.category: Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Attorney General Todd Blanche’s confirmation hearing before the Senate Judiciary Committee is scheduled for Wednesday.

item.title: Red State, Blue Governor: It Could Happen in Iowa. Would It Matter?
item.description: Democrats have real hope for candidates for governor in red states like Iowa and Ohio, but if Republicans seize supermajorities in the legislatures, their power would be limited.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen and Jamie Kelter Davis
item.media:description: Democrats want to block Republicans from winning a supermajority in the Iowa legislature in November, but the key will be winning over rural voters.

item.title: Can Democrats Win Rural Voters Turning Away From Trump? It’s a Tough Sell.
item.description: House candidates like Beth Macy, author of “Dopesick,” have crafted messages based on lifetimes in rural regions. But messages need an audience, and many minds are closed to the party.
item.dc:creator: Kate Zernike
item.media:credit: Matt Eich for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I think there’s a lot of rage, and feeling like people in power don’t care,” said the best-selling author Beth Macy, who is running for Congress as a Democrat.

item.title: How a Promising Navy Drone Boat Program Got Mired in Politics
item.description: Navy acquisition reforms upended a $2.1 billion contract competition. When it was over, several companies with ties to the Trump administration had made the cut.
item.dc:creator: Kate Kelly and Julie Tate
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: The Corsair, an unmanned vessel made by Saronic Technologies, demonstrated at a defense industry event last year. Saronic, which has twice made the Navy’s list of potential contract winners, has forged strong connections to allies of President Trump.
